region_of_origin: South India; widely served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general dosa entry; butter variant is a preparation sub-type not separately coded. Butter Dosai mapped to the parent dosa concept.
ontology.foodex2_description: Dosa — fermented rice and lentil pancake
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers plain dosa; butter variant is an adapted match. Butter addition is a preparation modifier not separately coded in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for dosa or butter dosai as of 2024.

serving.description: 1 piece of dosai with accompanying sambar and chutneys
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Butter Dosai is approximately 250 g, comprising one large thin crispy crepe with a generous butter coating, served alongside sambar and coconut chutney.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Dosa (fermented rice-lentil crepe) with butter adaptation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports plain dosa at approximately 149–155 kcal/100g. Butter Dosai values are adapted from the plain dosa baseline with butter addition increasing fat (particularly saturated fat), energy, and cholesterol. Values cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Food Composition Database for Singapore-context dosa entries. The per_100g values in the input (energy 152 kcal, protein 4.0 g, fat 7.2 g, carbohydrate 18.0 g) are consistent with ICMR-NIN plain dosa plus approximately 5–7 g butter per 100 g serving and are retained as the best available estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Dosa (fermented rice and lentil pancake)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 report plain dosa GI at approximately 51 (low GI). Butter Dosai is an adapted estimate: butter addition does not raise GI (fat slows gastric emptying, potentially lowering glycaemic response). GI of 51 is retained as a conservative estimate for Butter Dosai. GL per serving calculated as: GI (51) × carbohydrate per serving (45 g from 250 g × 18 g/100g) / 100 = 22.95, rounded to 22.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 260 mg/100g is borderline; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 650 mg sodium, representing 28% of the 2300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Butter Dosai to one piece per meal, request reduced salt in sambar and chutneys, and avoid adding extra butter to keep total sodium and saturated fat within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate at 18 g/100g with a low GI of approximately 51; a 250 g serving provides 45 g carbohydrate with a glycaemic load of approximately 23.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes may consume one Butter Dosai per meal as part of a controlled carbohydrate plan, pairing it with sambar (lentil protein and fibre) to further blunt the glycaemic response, while monitoring total carbohydrate intake across the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 4.0 g/100g and trans fat at 0.2 g/100g from butter; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 10 g saturated fat and 0.5 g trans fat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request a reduced butter application or substitute with a minimal amount of plant-based oil to lower saturated and trans fat intake, and limit serving to one piece to keep saturated fat within the 7% of daily energy target.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat at 4.0 g/100g is below the 5 g/100g RA threshold; fermented batter provides probiotic benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may consume Butter Dosai in moderation, requesting lighter butter application to keep saturated fat below threshold, and confirming sambar is tomato-free if nightshade sensitivity is a concern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Butter Dosai is a popular South Indian fermented crepe widely served in Singapore's Indian hawker stalls and restaurants. It is made from a batter of fermented rice and urad dal (black lentil), spread thinly on a hot iron griddle and cooked until golden and crispy, then finished with a generous application of butter. The result is a thin, lacy, crispy crepe with a slightly tangy fermented flavour from the lactic acid fermentation process, a rich buttery aroma, and a delicate crunch that gives way to a soft interior. It is traditionally served with sambar (a spiced lentil and vegetable soup) and coconut chutney. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 152 kcal, 4.0 g protein, 7.2 g fat (4.0 g saturated from butter), 18.0 g carbohydrate, 1.6 g dietary fibre, and 260 mg sodium. The fermented batter has a lower glycaemic index (approximately 51, low GI) compared to plain rice, due to lactic acid fermentation and the protein contribution of urad dal. A standard serving of 250 g delivers approximately 45 g carbohydrate and a glycaemic load of about 23. Clinically, sodium content is borderline for hypertension management, saturated fat from butter warrants attention for cardiovascular disease patients, and portion control is advised for Type 2 Diabetes. It is vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary preferences in Singapore's multicultural food landscape.
common_user_queries: How many calories in butter dosai?
Is butter dosa good for diabetics?
What is the GI of dosa?
Can I eat butter dosai with hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain dosa data with butter addition adjustment, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Food Composition Database. No exact Butter Dosai entry exists in a single approved primary source; values are internally consistent and plausible but represent an adapted estimate rather than a direct measurement.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile (monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown), potassium, full B-vitamin panel (B1, B2, B3, B6, B12, folate), fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E), minerals (phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium), and moisture data for butter dosai as a composite dish are not separately reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods or HPB Singapore Food Composition Database as of 2024; no approved source consulted provides these values for this specific preparation. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.2 g/100g based on butter content (butter contains trace trans fats from natural ruminant sources, consistent with USDA FoodData Central butter data). calcium_mg (32.0), iron_mg (0.8), cholesterol_mg (12.0), and vitamin_c_mg (2.0) are retained from the input record, consistent with ICMR-NIN dosa data ranges. GI of 51 is an analogous estimate from Atkinson et al. 2021 (University of Sydney GI Database) for plain fermented dosa; butter addition is not expected to raise GI. GL per serving = 51 × 45 / 100 = 22.95. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03302051 maps to the general dosa concept; butter variant is a preparation sub-type. FoodEx2 code A0CKP is an exact match for fermented rice-lentil pancake with butter as a preparation modifier. No SNOMED CT code exists for dosa as of 2024.
